papers.title: Butyrate-mediated HDAC inhibition restores intestinal barrier integrity via TLR4/NF-kappaB pathway suppression in a porcine colitis model
papers.abstract: We investigated the role of microbially derived butyrate in maintaining intestinal barrier function using a DSS colitis model in weaned piglets. Butyrate enemas (100 mM) significantly reduced intestinal permeability and upregulated tight junction proteins (ZO-1, occludin, claudin-4). Mechanistically, butyrate inhibited HDAC activity (HDAC3/8), resulting in increased histone H3K27 acetylation at ZO-1 and occludin promoters, while simultaneously suppressing TLR4/NF-kappaB nuclear translocation. Multi-compartment sampling (luminal, epithelial, and subepithelial) revealed a 100-fold butyrate concentration gradient. RNA-seq and ChIP-seq confirmed epigenetic regulation of barrier genes.
papers.keyLimitation: Luminal vs. intracellular butyrate concentration gradient (100-fold) measured but not causally linked to differential HDAC inhibition; single time-point sampling.
papers.summary: Strong porcine in vivo evidence that butyrate maintains barrier integrity through HDAC3/8 inhibition and TLR4/NF-kappaB suppression. Notable for measuring multi-compartment butyrate gradients.

papers.title: Hyodeoxycholic acid activates intestinal FXR to promote Lactobacillus expansion and enhance barrier function in weaned piglets
papers.abstract: Hyodeoxycholic acid (HDCA), the dominant bile acid in porcine intestine, was administered to weaned piglets (50 mg/kg/day, 14 days). HDCA supplementation significantly increased Lactobacillus abundance from 5.3% to 38.0% (P < 0.001) and enhanced intestinal barrier function. Mechanistic studies using FXR-knockout pig intestinal organoids confirmed FXR-PI3K/AKT pathway involvement. Whether Lactobacillus expansion is a direct effect or secondary to barrier improvement was not determined.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine in vivo (weaned piglets) + FXR-KO organoids
papers.keyLimitation: FXR activation and Lactobacillus shift measured in same animal but temporal asynchrony not characterized; single-compartment sampling only.
papers.summary: Porcine in vivo demonstration that HDCA, the dominant porcine bile acid, promotes Lactobacillus expansion through FXR-PI3K/AKT. Uses porcine-specific bile acid rather than murine-dominant bile acids.

papers.title: Bacteroides thetaiotaomicron tryptophan metabolism activates AhR-Nrf2 signaling to suppress pyroptosis and maintain intestinal epithelial homeostasis in piglets
papers.abstract: B. thetaiotaomicron mono-colonization in germ-free piglets significantly reduced intestinal epithelial cell pyroptosis compared to GF controls. Metabolomic profiling identified tryptophan-derived indole-3-propionic acid (IPA) as the primary enriched metabolite. In IPEC-J2 cells, IPA activated AhR nuclear translocation, inducing Nrf2 response and suppressing NLRP3 inflammasome assembly. AhR antagonist CH223191 abolished the protective effect.
papers.keyLimitation: B. thetaiotaomicron strain-specific; purified IPA not validated in vivo; single-compartment measurement only.
papers.summary: First GF porcine demonstration that B. thetaiotaomicron-derived IPA suppresses pyroptosis via AhR-Nrf2. Advances tryptophan node forward pathway to L2a in porcine model.

papers.title: Gut microbial propionate production is vitamin B12-dependent: Methylmalonyl-CoA mutase requirement links one-carbon metabolism to SCFA output in humanized pig microbiota
papers.abstract: Using ex vivo porcine cecal batch cultures, we demonstrated that propionate production is strictly vitamin B12-dependent. B12 supplementation increased propionate 4.2-fold while reducing succinate by 80%. Metagenomic analysis revealed Phascolarctobacterium succinatutens ASVs increased 12-fold. In silico genome analysis confirmed complete B12-dependent methylmalonyl-CoA pathway (scpA/scpB/scpC operon). Entirely ex vivo; in situ validation has not been performed.
papers.modelSystem: Ex vivo porcine cecal batch cultures
papers.keyLimitation: Exclusively ex vivo; no in vivo B12 supplementation study in pigs; B12 concentration gradient from lumen to epithelium not measured.
papers.summary: Demonstrates B12-dependence of propionate production through methylmalonyl-CoA mutase in porcine-derived microbiota. Links B12 node to Butyrate/SCFAs node via P. succinatutens.

papers.title: Polyamine metabolism in the porcine gut: baseline concentrations, spatial distribution, and microbial biosynthetic gene abundance along the intestinal tract
papers.abstract: We characterized polyamine concentrations (putrescine, spermidine, spermine, cadaverine) in five intestinal compartments of 24 commercial crossbred pigs at market weight. Luminal polyamine concentrations ranged from 0.5-3.2 mM, highest in cecum and proximal colon. Metagenomic analysis identified polyamine biosynthetic gene clusters predominantly in Bacteroidetes and Firmicutes. No strain isolation or mono-colonization experiments were performed.
papers.modelSystem: Commercial pigs (Landrace x Yorkshire x Duroc)
papers.keyLimitation: No causal validation; no strain isolation; no intervention experiment; purely descriptive survey.
papers.summary: First comprehensive baseline of porcine intestinal polyamine concentrations across five compartments. Essential reference data but no causal evidence. Polyamine node remains at L1 in porcine models.

papers.title: FXR activation by obeticholic acid alters gut microbial composition independently of bile acid pool changes: evidence for direct host-to-microbiome signaling
papers.abstract: In a mouse model with hepatocyte-specific FXR overexpression, we observed significant shifts in cecal microbial community composition without detectable changes in total bile acid pool. RNA-seq identified FXR-dependent upregulation of antimicrobial peptides (Reg3gamma, Reg3beta, Ang4). This represents the first evidence for a potential bile acid-independent host FXR-to-microbiome communication pathway (Reverse pathway, L3 in mice). Porcine relevance remains untested.
papers.keyLimitation: Murine model only; porcine bile acid composition fundamentally differs (HDCA-dominant vs murine); reverse pathway in pigs at L0.
papers.summary: First evidence for bile acid-independent FXR-to-microbiome signaling (host FXR -> antimicrobial peptides -> microbiota). Important for bile acid reverse pathway (L0 in pigs). Clear porcine translational gap.

papers.title: Butyrate promotes macrophage H4K31 crotonylation through mTORC1-HDAC3/8 pathway in Tibetan pig intestinal inflammation
papers.abstract: In Tibetan pigs with DSS-induced colitis, butyrate supplementation ameliorated disease activity and promoted macrophage alternative activation. Quantitative crotonylomics identified 1,247 H4K31 crotonylation sites significantly altered by butyrate, with mTORC1-HDAC3/8 as the regulatory axis. First demonstration of butyrate-mediated histone crotonylation in a porcine model, extending the known epigenetic repertoire beyond acetylation.
papers.keyLimitation: Tibetan pig breed-specific; single-compartment tissue homogenate; no time-series data; crotonylation functional significance not proven.
papers.summary: First demonstration of butyrate-mediated H4K31 crotonylation in porcine intestine, expanding butyrate's epigenetic repertoire beyond acetylation. Adds novel mechanism to butyrate forward pathway.

papers.title: Spermidine supplementation during the weaning transition: effects on intestinal morphology, microbial diversity, and systemic metabolite profiles in piglets
papers.abstract: Weaned piglets (n=48) received dietary spermidine supplementation (0, 0.3, or 1.0 mmol/kg diet) for 28 days. High-dose spermidine increased jejunal villus height:crypt depth ratio (P=0.02) and altered microbial beta-diversity. Plasma metabolomics identified 34 significantly altered metabolites. No microbial polyamine producers were identified and no mechanistic experiments were conducted.
papers.keyLimitation: Dietary intervention without microbial strain identification or mechanistic experiments; cannot distinguish host vs microbial polyamine origin.
papers.summary: Dietary spermidine study in weaned piglets showing intestinal morphology improvement. Correlation-level (L1) evidence only. Polyamine node forward pathway remains entirely at L1.
